runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 6 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Congressional Budget Office (2012). How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in the After-Tax Wage.
McClelland and Mok (2012). A Review of Recent Research on Labor Supply Elasticities. CBO Working Paper.
runs.reasoningSummary: In U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation models like those used by the CBO, primary earners generally exhibit lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. For middle-income groups such as earnings decile 6, a standard substitution elasticity typically ranges between 0.15 and 0.25, centering around 0.20 based on broad consensus estimates in labor economics.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the middle of the income distribution (such as decile 6) generally exhibit low substitution elasticities because they are typically attached to the labor market with full-time hours. Consensus estimates used in major U.S. microsimulation models (like those from the CBO) typically place the primary-earner substitution elasticity between 0.1 and 0.25.
